summary: Gruber responds to Mark Gurman's Bloomberg report about Apple being behind in AI, pushing back on the narrative that Apple has entirely missed the boat. He argues that Apple's improved autocorrect in iOS 17 and AI-powered Photos search are genuinely significant but underappreciated achievements. While acknowledging that Apple wouldn't make anyone's list of AI-leading companies, he reframes the concern: the real anxiety inside Apple isn't about being late to AI generally, but about whether Apple's internal AI/ML team can deliver competitive results given the company's insistence on building everything in-house, partly for privacy reasons.
key_points: Apple's iOS 17 autocorrect improvement is a major AI achievement that's underrated because it's unglamorous — it's used by nearly every iOS user in nearly every app, every day
Apple is almost never first to adopt new technologies; their deliberate approach aims to integrate new tech best, not first
The real internal anxiety at Apple isn't about having missed AI, but about whether Apple's own AI/ML team can deliver results competitive with the industry leaders
Apple's insistence on using only its own AI/ML work — driven partly by privacy commitments — creates a bottleneck that worries employees
AI integration is becoming a core competitive dimension alongside hardware manufacturing and interface design
money_quotes: I would argue that the improved autocorrect in iOS 17 is a major feature — in my use it's clearly an improvement, and autocorrect is a feature used every day, in almost every app, by almost every iOS user.
If I asked you "Which companies are at the forefront of AI-powered products?", I doubt you'd put Apple on the list.
AI is proving so useful — and yet is a nascent field — that Apple needs to soon be on that list, lest their products begin to fall behind competitively.
"Which companies are best at integrating AI into products?" is going to be like "Which companies are best at creating hardware at scale?" and "Which companies are best at human interface design?"
Apple is almost never at the forefront of stuff like this. They're a deliberate company. Their goal, as with any new technology, is to integrate it into products in meaningful ways best, not first.

The anxiety inside Apple is that many people inside do not believe Apple's own AI/ML team can deliver, but that the company — if only for privacy reasons — will only use what comes from its own AI/ML team.
key_insight: Apple's real AI problem isn't being late to the party — it's that the company's insistence on only using its own AI/ML work may prevent it from ever catching up if its internal team can't match the competition.
